item.title: Oil prices plunge after Trump says talks have been held to end war
item.description: Energy prices fall and stock markets rebound after the US president says "very good and productive" talks have been held.

item.title: Iran War Live Updates: Trump Says U.S. and Iran Held ‘Very Good’ Talks on Ending Conflict
item.description: President Trump said the two countries had “productive conversations regarding a complete and total resolution of our hostilities in the Middle East.” Iran did not immediately comment.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
Trump looking at inviting Belarus’s Lukashenko to White House
item.description: US administration seeks diplomatic thaw with authoritarian ally of Vladimir Putin

item.title: London Arson Attack on Jewish Ambulance Service Investigated as Hate Crime, Police Say
item.description: The city’s main police force said it was investigating fires that struck four ambulances as an antisemitic hate crime.

item.title: Slovenia’s liberal premier claims narrow victory over Trump ally
item.description: Prime Minister Robert Golob faces protracted coalition talks after edging populist challenger Janez Janša

item.title: London ambulances set on fire in suspected antisemitic incident
item.description: Keir Starmer condemns ‘horrific’ attack on vehicles belonging to charity in north London

item.title: Lionel Jospin, former French Socialist prime minister, dies at 88
item.description: Jospin’s government introduced 35-hour working week and pushed privatisation of state-owned companies

item.title: Taiwan Debates Military Spending as Choices Over U.S. and China Loom
item.description: Tensions in the opposition Nationalist Party could surface this week as lawmakers argue over additional defense funding intended to counter Beijing’s growing might.

item.title: FirstFT: Stocks fall on Trump’s Iran ultimatum
item.description: Also in today’s newsletter: LaGuardia closed after collision at airport and European elections latest

item.title: Gilt yields surge to highest level since 2008
item.description: Traders bet on four Bank of England rate rises this year

item.title: Iranians Fear Trump’s Threat to Strike Power Plants
item.description: With President Trump threatening to strike Iranian power plants, some war-weary civilians are panicking over a possible new debacle.

item.title: Why the BTS Comeback Concert Was a ‘Disaster’ for Some Businesses
item.description: The turnout for the K-pop titans’ show was much lower than projected by officials, hitting the bottom line of some restaurants. Shares in the group’s management company also fell.

item.title: Larry Fink warns AI may intensify wealth inequality
item.description: CEO of world’s biggest asset manager says rich backers of artificial intelligence could reap most of the rewards

item.title: Sidelined by War With Iran, Gaza Residents Remain in Limbo
item.description: The new war has led to panic buying and a surge in food prices for Gazans as they try to recover from Israel’s two-year offensive against Hamas.

item.title: A Choice of Deadly Drones Is Only a Few Clicks Away for Ukrainian Troops
item.description: Ukraine has created online marketplaces to let units select their own drones, a break from generations of standardized and centralized weapons procurement.

item.title: Ministers cannot wish away higher costs by blaming ‘profiteering’
item.description: Middle East war has meant richer countries are buying more limited supplies of oil and gas at higher prices

item.title: Kenyan police investigate alleged disappearance of ex-foreign minister
item.description: The reported disappearance of Raphael Tuju has led to claims he may have been abducted.

item.title: Starmer and Trump discuss need to reopen Hormuz Strait
item.description: No 10 says the leaders agree reopening the shipping lane is key to ensuring energy market stability.

item.title: Australia's ABC staff to go on strike for first time in 20 years
item.description: The 24-hour long strike is expected to disrupt live television and radio broadcasts.

item.title: Berkshire Hathaway to take $1.8bn stake in Tokio Marine
item.description: Investment in non-life insurer marks new Japan venture for Warren Buffett’s conglomerate

item.title: French Far Right Falls Short of Statement Win in Yardstick Local Races
item.description: France’s far right hoped for major gains in Sunday’s municipal elections, a key bellwether moment before a presidential election next year. Its results were mixed.

item.title: Energy Attacks in War on Iran Could Turn Economic Shock Into Long-Term Damage
item.description: A new phase targeting oil and gas infrastructure in the Persian Gulf threatens to hurt businesses and customers around the world for months or even years.

item.title: Canada May Need to Lean on the US as Military Threats in the Arctic Rise
item.description: For the past seven decades, Canada has been the junior partner in a military agreement with the United States to protect the Canadian Arctic.

item.title: Canada’s Military Wants to Prove It Can Defend the High Arctic
item.description: Canadian soldiers transported M777 howitzers to the High Arctic to show their ability to fight in an increasingly contested part of the world. It did not go as planned.

item.title: Diabetes, Overlooked and Unchecked, Poses New Risks in Africa
item.description: As deaths from diabetes start to rival those from infectious threats like malaria, a new form of the condition linked to malnutrition is surfacing in patients who can afford neither screening nor care.

item.title: Starmer grapples with Iran economic fallout and Trump mockery
item.description: UK ministers to discuss response to energy prices after US president shares ‘Saturday Night Live’ sketch

item.title: HK police can now demand phone passwords under new national security rules
item.description: Those who do not comply can be jailed for up to a year and fined up to HK$100,000 ($12,700; £9,600).

item.title: Gilts: blame the hedge funds?
item.description: No one’s doing it like the Brits

item.title: Plan to scrap most short jail terms comes into effect
item.description: The government hopes it will ease the population crisis in prisons.

item.title: Wall Street’s big private capital problem
item.description: Plus, Victory boosts bid for Janus, private capital’s financing wheeze and Giant hits Broadway

item.title: EU to clinch trade deal with Australia after eight years of talks
item.description: Also in this newsletter: Greek shipowner questions EU sanctions on Russia amid energy price rise

item.title: China battery trio gain $70bn as Iran war sparks ‘paradigm shift’
item.description: Share price rises for clean energy companies outstrip oil majors as investors bet on switch to renewables

item.title: Danish PM rides Trump Greenland row into election resurgence
item.description: Standing up to US president has boosted Mette Frederiksen’s chances of winning third term

item.title: Trump’s Iran playbook was written in the 1980s
item.description: From oil threats to ultimatums, the US president has a decades-old view of how to confront Tehran

item.title: Starmer to bring forward EU rules legislation in King’s Speech
item.description: Some Labour officials hope bill will provoke Brexit dispute with Tories and Reform UK

item.title: London police chief warns of ‘sustained threat’ from Iranian state plots
item.description: Mark Rowley issues warning during US visit and also accuses ‘hostile states’ of spreading false claims that UK capital is unsafe

item.title: America keeps bailing out Trump
item.description: The country’s underlying strengths are absorbing his erratic choices, including the war on Iran

item.title: Iran war is a risk to the flow of Gulf funds around the globe
item.description: World has come to depend on capital from the region more deeply than many realise

item.title: Caught Between Two Conflicts, Afghans Flee Iran
item.description: The war in Iran is choking off Afghanistan’s main economic lifeline and has forced at least 70,000 Afghan workers and students there back home to a nation embroiled in another conflict.

item.title: BTS agency shares drop after comeback show turnout falls short
item.description: The concert was attended by an estimated 104,000 fans, much lower than the expected 260,000.

item.title: Trump’s Ultimatum to Iran
item.description: The president’s messages on the war have shifted, but the latest threat might be a sign that he’s moving to a harder stance.

item.title: In Denmark’s Election, How Will the Woman Who Took On Trump Fare?
item.description: Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen is the biggest force this country has seen in decades. The crisis in Greenland has energized her, but are voters itching for change?

item.title: Israel’s Missile Defense Under Scrutiny After Iranian Attack
item.description: Two missiles landed hours apart, wreaking havoc in two towns near a heavily guarded nuclear site in the Negev Desert.

item.title: Germany has a shortage of workers - so it's turning to India for help
item.description: The European nation, struggling to find skilled staff, is giving jobs to young people from India.

item.title: France's Socialists hold on to power in major cities in election boost for mainstream
item.description: Paris and Marseille are held by the Socialists in local elections which saw the nationalist right win in Nice and certain towns.

item.title: Ministers name seven areas where 'new towns' could be built
item.description: Six sites have been deprioritised from the flagship scheme to build a wave of new settlements.

item.title: Israeli settlers target Palestinian villages in occupied West Bank, attacking people and properties
item.description: The violence began after 18-year-old settler Yehuda Sherman was killed after reportedly being hit by a vehicle driven by a Palestinian while on his quad bike.

item.title: National blackout hits Cuba for second time in a week
item.description: Another total failure of its power grid underlines Cuba's hardship under a US-imposed fuel blockade.

item.title: Why did US and Israel attack Iran and how long could the war last?
item.description: Conflict has spread across the Middle East since the US and Israel launched strikes on Iran on 28 February.

item.title: Israel to expand ground and air attacks against Hezbollah in Lebanon
item.description: There are fears in Lebanon that Israel is preparing for a large-scale ground invasion.

item.title: The Billionaire Funding France’s Far Right
item.description: Pierre-Édouard Stérin is financing projects to make France less Muslim, more Catholic and more capitalist. He says his program has trained thousands running for municipal office on Sunday.

item.title: Russian attacks kill six in Ukraine, officials say
item.description: The deaths were reported in four south-eastern Ukrainian regions in the past 24 hours.

item.title: Ukraine’s top drone commander wants to bleed Russia’s army dry
item.description: “Madyar” Brovdi has pioneered unmanned warfare methods NATO is yet to learn

item.title: Donald Trump has four bad options for the war in Iran
item.description: None is guaranteed to end the war or ease the economic crisis it has produced

item.title: Kenyans fighting illegally for Russia in Ukraine to be granted amnesty
item.description: It is illegal for Kenyans to serve in foreign armies and can carry a sentence of up to 10 years in jail.

item.title: Tributes paid to former Sunday Game presenter Michael Lyster
item.description: Michael Lyster was best known for presenting The Sunday Game Live for more than 30 years.

item.title: No assessment Iran could strike London, UK minister says
item.description: Steve Reed says there is "no specific assessment" Iran is capable of hitting the UK, after Israel claimed it now could.

item.title: Iranian missiles injure 180 in towns near Israeli nuclear site
item.description: Israel is investigating how ballistic missiles got through the country's sophisticated air defences.

item.title: Why Bangalore has India’s best billionaires
item.description: Its philanthropists want to do more than just give money away

item.title: Attack on a Sudan Hospital Kills Dozens, Head of W.H.O. Says
item.description: The group’s director general said 13 children were among those killed in the latest violence in the Darfur region, and he decried the targeting of health care facilities in the civil war.

item.title: Qatari and Turkish citizens die in military helicopter crash
item.description: Four Qatari military personnel, one Turkish serviceman and two technicians from a Turkish defence company were on board.

item.title: Sudan drone attack on key hospital killed 64 people during Eid, WHO says
item.description: The strike killed 64 people, including 13 children, two nurses and a doctor, according to the WHO head.

item.title: How a medieval English abbey became a Chinese wedding destination
item.description: Today's brides say they are inspired by wedding of Taiwanese popstar Jay Chou at the abbey in 2015.

item.title: On Beirut’s Waterfront, Loss Meets Life, and Luxury, Amid War
item.description: Thousands displaced by Israeli strikes on Lebanon’s capital now shelter along the promenade hugging the Mediterranean Sea. They share it with joggers, cyclists and dog walkers, alongside dizzying displays of wealth.

item.title: Foreign secretary denounces 'reckless Iran threats' after missiles fired at Diego Garcia
item.description: Iran reportedly fired two ballistic missiles at Indian Ocean base, but neither reached the target.

item.title: 'Peace is a gradual thing': How land, cattle and identity fuel a deadly Nigerian conflict
item.description: How a lack of justice and trust in the security forces perpetuates deadly violence in Plateau state.

item.title: Trump at a crossroads as US weighs tough options in Iran
item.description: Statements from President Trump that the war is almost over are not matching up with the reality on the ground.

item.title: Italy is voting on whether to change its constitution. What does this mean for Meloni?
item.description: The dry subject matter of this weekend's vote seems obscure but for many it is a vote on the PM.

item.title: Pop megastars BTS electrify historic centre of Seoul with comeback concert
item.description: The world's biggest band returns after more than three years with a huge concert in the South Korean capital.

item.title: Did Australia's under-16s social media ban work?
item.description: Three months since it came into effect, teenagers tell the BBC how the world-first law has impacted their lives so far.

item.title: Rwanda batter becomes youngest women's T20 centurion
item.description: Rwanda's Fanny Utagushimaninde says it was like "a dream" after becoming the youngest woman to make a T20 international century at the age of 15 years and 223 days.

item.title: Why there's no sign of the conflict in the Middle East 'winding down'
item.description: Iran's foreign minister said that it doesn't appear that the US is prepared to halt its aggression after President Trump said the US was considering "winding down" its war.

item.title: BTS make live return in front of huge crowd
item.description: RM, Jin, Suga, J-Hope, Jimin, V and Jung Kook perform together for the first time since October 2022.

item.title: US lifts sanctions on some Iranian oil as energy prices soar
item.description: The treasury secretary says the move will quickly bring about 140m barrels of oil to global markets.

item.title: UK allows US to use bases to strike Strait of Hormuz targets
item.description: Downing Street says ministers approved the expansion after accusing Iran of "reckless strikes".

item.title: BTS Arirang review: K-pop idols rekindle their fire
item.description: The band's 10th album sees them return to the rebellious, rap-heavy sound of their early days.

item.title: Watch: Thick smoke billows from South Korea car parts plant in deadly fire
item.description: Eleven people were killed and dozens injured when a huge blaze engulfed a car parts factory in the central city of Daejeon.

item.title: Russia's school propaganda was highlighted by Oscar-winning film - but does it work?
item.description: The messages are clear: the full-scale invasion is a defensive war and patriotism means unquestionable loyalty.

item.title: The forgotten Indian woman trailblazer in British medicine
item.description: In 1912, Jamini Sen became the first woman Fellow of Glasgow’s Royal College, founded in 1599 and long closed to women.

item.title: Iranian strikes on bases used by US caused $800m in damage, new analysis shows
item.description: Much of the damage was caused in initial retaliatory strikes by Iran in the week after the US and Israel launched its operation.

item.title: Ukraine-Hungary oil pipeline row threatens EU loan
item.description: Kyiv and Budapest disagree on how badly damaged a hub in western Ukraine bombed by Russia really is, as its oil flow remains suspended.

item.title: Starmer most popular PM - in Toby Jug sales
item.description: Toby Jugs depicting Sir Keir outsold all other prime ministers in the Parliament shop in 2025.

item.title: Reform UK suspends Scotland candidate over financial allegations
item.description: Scotland leader Malcolm Offord told BBC Radio Scotland that the party had "spent a lot of time on vetting" candidates

item.title: Farage 'will fight' Essex council reorganisation
item.description: A senior Conservative councillor says the Reform UK leader "knows nothing" about local government.

item.title: Hillsborough Law to be further delayed
item.description: The prime minister had promised to pass the legislation by 15 April 2025, to mark the 36th anniversary of the disaster.

item.title: Border security chief to step down after 18 months in the job
item.description: Martin Hewitt had been tasked with cutting the numbers trying to get to the UK in small boats.

item.title: Israel strikes Syria after Druze clashes
item.description: Syria condemned the strikes on weapons sites and government infrastructure as an "outrageous" assault on Syria's sovereignty.

item.title: Neighbourhoods to get £20m funding boost
item.description: Local people can help decide where the £20m over a decade should go, the government says.

item.title: Norway's crown princess breaks silence, claiming she was 'manipulated and deceived' by Epstein
item.description: Crown Princess Mette Marit tells Norwegian TV she wishes she had never met the late sex offender.

item.title: UK borrowing costs hit highest since 2008 financial crisis
item.description: The interest rate on government debt is climbing over fears about inflation, interest rates, and public spending, experts have said.

item.title: Iran warns UK letting US use bases is 'participation in aggression'
item.description: Iran's foreign minister urges his UK counterpart Yvette Cooper to end cooperation with the US.

item.title: Car stolen with two-month-old baby inside
item.description: The baby boy was rescued by police unharmed and reunited with his mother shortly after the ordeal.

item.title: Leading anti‑racism activist in Tunisia jailed for eight years
item.description: Saadia Mosbah campaigned for migrants, particularly after President Kais Saied said they posed a demographic threat to the country.

item.title: Reform-led council approves councillor wage rise
item.description: It is "bad optics" to "award ourselves a nice little pay rise", a Green Party councillor says.

item.title: Venezuelan leader replaces senior military commanders
item.description: The reshuffle follows Delcy Rodríguez's appointment of a new defence minister, a move human rights groups described as a "recycling of impunity".
